Dungeon Crawler Carl (Dungeon Crawler Carl, #1)
Dinniman, Matt *
059382024X
9780593820247
059382024X
450 pp
4.46
396,418
Sep 21, 2020
Aug 27, 2024
1 of 5 stars2 of 5 stars3 of 5 stars[ 4 of 5 stars ]5 of 5 stars
read, 2026
[edit]
**spoiler alert** I listened to this one right after The Count of Monte Cristo, deliberately looking for something I could be less tapped into. Dungeo **spoiler alert** I listened to this one right after The Count of Monte Cristo, deliberately looking for something I could be less tapped into. Dungeon Crawler was a good fit. It's easy to keep coming back to without requiring much from you.

The strongest part for me was the progression: stat allocation, item rewards, new mechanics being introduced, the funny achievements. I basically experienced it the way I experience Civ 6 or TF2. I don't need a lot of backstory or narrative context, I just want to understand the mechanics and progress. That probably says something about why the systems side of this book appealed to me more than the characters did.

The scenes that worked best were the ones that felt genuinely novel to this book, especially the roly-poly wheel and the rage elemental. The other fights felt comparatively boring. The talk show segments didn't really do it for me either, and I would have liked more depth on the actual stats and abilities. But those are relatively minor complaints for a book I was mostly just enjoying passively. ...more [edit]

remove book
The Count of Monte Cristo
Dumas, Alexandre
0140449264
9780140449266
0140449264
1,276 pp
4.33
1,053,689
1846
Mar 27, 2003
1 of 5 stars2 of 5 stars3 of 5 stars4 of 5 stars[ 5 of 5 stars ]
read, 2026
[edit]
**spoiler alert** A very engaging novel, despite its length. The prison sections were phenomenal because it genuinely felt like Edmond's soul and futu **spoiler alert** A very engaging novel, despite its length. The prison sections were phenomenal because it genuinely felt like Edmond's soul and future were hanging in the balance. The moment Edmond first communicated with Abbe Faria through the prison wall was probably the high point of the novel for me. After so much isolation and hopelessness, that scene felt genuinely sublime. Weirdly, Edmond's transformation in prison after meeting the Abbe ended up feeling far more satisfying than the actual revenge plot that follows.

My main issue is that the second half slowly loses tension once Edmond becomes “The Count” and turns into more of an untouchable force than a person. The novel keeps hinting that his revenge is morally dangerous (he's trying to act as Providence and becoming something almost Satanic) but never fully follows through on the consequences of that idea. The death of Villefort's child feels like Dumas wanting us to question Edmond's revenge but pulling back from the full moral implications of it.

I honestly think the novel would have become a masterpiece for me if someone like Valentine had died because of Edmond's actions and forced him to confront the destructiveness of orienting his entire life around vengeance. I spent a lot of the second half feeling like the novel was building toward a psychological reckoning. I expected that when Edmond revisited Chateau d'If, he would realize that his transformation there alongside the Abbe had actually been the most meaningful period of his life and not the revenge itself. Instead, the “wait and hope” ending felt unearned to me because Edmond didn’t exactly wait and hope; he destroyed.

Still a fantastic book overall, just not quite the tragedy I thought it was building toward. ...more [edit]
None [edit]
